lesson_title: Compare Fractions with the Same Denominator
objective: Compare two fractions with the same denominator using models and symbols (>, <, =)

pretest.question: Sam ate 5/8 of a pie. Kai ate 2/8 of the same pie. Who ate more?

learn.instruction: When two fractions have the SAME DENOMINATOR, the pieces are the same size. You just compare how many pieces each fraction has — that's the numerator. More pieces = greater fraction. Example: 2/8 vs 6/8 → same size pieces, but 6 pieces > 2 pieces, so 6/8 > 2/8.
learn.question: Which is greater: 3/6 or 5/6?

learn.instruction: You can use a fraction strip to see this. Imagine a bar split into 4 equal parts. Color 1 part → 1/4. Color 3 parts → 3/4. The bar with 3 parts colored is clearly more filled. So 3/4 > 1/4.
learn.question: A bar is split into 8 equal parts. One shows 2/8 colored, another shows 7/8 colored. Which is greater?

learn.instruction: If both fractions have the same numerator AND the same denominator, they are equal. Example: 3/4 = 3/4. This also works in word problems: if two people each eat 2/6 of the same cake, they ate the same amount.
learn.question: Compare: 5/8 ○ 5/8
learn.explanation: Same denominator, same numerator → equal fractions.

try.question: Look at the fraction strips. Strip A shows 3/8 shaded. Strip B shows 6/8 shaded. Compare: 3/8 ○ 6/8
guided_steps: Step 1: Both strips are split into 8 equal parts (same denominator).
Step 2: Strip A has 3 parts shaded. Strip B has 6 parts shaded.
hints: Count the shaded parts. Which strip has more shaded?

try.question: Ella used 4/6 of her paint. Mia used 1/6 of the same paint. Who used more paint? Write a comparison.

practice_r1.question: Jess walked 5/8 of a path. Tom walked 4/8 of the same path. Who walked farther?

practice_r2.question: Two same-size boards. Board A has 3/4 painted. Board B has 2/4 painted. Which board has more paint? Write a comparison.
Write a fraction with denominator 6 that is LESS than 5/6.

practice_r3.question: Leo says 2/8 > 5/8 because 2 comes first when you count. Is Leo correct? Explain.
practice_r3.correct_answer: Leo is NOT correct. With the same denominator, the greater numerator means more pieces. 5 > 2, so 5/8 > 2/8.

practice_r3.question: Write ALL the fractions with denominator 4 (from 0 to 4 in the numerator) in order from least to greatest.
Ava, Ben, and Cal each have the same-size granola bar. Ava ate 6/8, Ben ate 3/8, and Cal ate 5/8. List who ate the most to the least.
